old_claim.note: Satellite Technology provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Spaceborne optical instruments require a satellite or spacecraft platform; TIROS tested optical payloads, data handling, and spacecraft operations from orbit.

source_shape.source_locator: NASA describes TIROS-1 as a satellite designed to test whether Earth could be studied from space and says the TIROS program tested spacecraft instruments, data, and operational parameters.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The first scoped spaceborne optical instrument was a satellite mission with spacecraft-mounted instruments and data-handling operations.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the corrected node is specifically spaceborne, absence of a satellite or spacecraft platform breaks the claim even though later optical payloads vary.

ontology_before: Space-Based Advanced Optics treated satellite technology as a generic enabling capability.
ontology_after: Spaceborne Optical Instruments requires satellite technology as the platform that carries and operates the optical payload from orbit.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: satellite_technology is now required rather than enabling.
would_reject_if: The node is later broadened to include airborne or ground-based advanced optics.
A separate non-satellite high-altitude optical platform node replaces the current spaceborne scope.
The scope is narrowed to optical components independent of any spacecraft platform.
short_reason: A spaceborne optical payload needs a spacecraft platform.
